message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 8
Recent changes: Green phase added NO production code — the "4 runes → 115" test was a boundary/triangulation test already satisfied by the existing `runeCount === 3 ? block : count×25` logic.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, APP mass before/after. Since no code changed this cycle, just confirm the current code is clean or make any small improvement justified by current tests. Do not implement ahead of tests (mixed component types / multiple blocks are not yet tested).

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 8 tests pass. Now let me work through the refactoring process.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing each name against current behavior (8 passing tests: empty list, single item base premiums, 2/3/4 runes):

- `runScenario` — runs a scenario, maps steps to results. Clear.
- `quotePremium` — computes premium for a quote step. Clear.
- `policyBasePremium` — sums base premiums for items in a policy. Clear.
- `runesBasePremium` — computes base premium for the rune component. Clear.
- `RUNE_BLOCK_PREMIUM` (60) — the block price for 3 runes. Clear.
- `runeCount`, `nonRuneBase`, `policyBase`, `firstInsurance` — all reveal intent well.

**Decision**: Keep all current names. They reveal intent accurately for the behavior tested so far.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focusing on the rune-handling logic, which is where any conceptual question lives.

`runesBasePremium`:
- Bindings: `runeCount`, `RUNE_BLOCK_PREMIUM`, `BASE_PREMIUM.rune` = 3
- Constants: `3` = 1
- Invocations: `*` = 2
- Conditionals: `?:` = 4
- Total: **10**

`policyBasePremium`:
- Bindings: `items`, `runeCount`, `nonRuneBase`, `item`(×3), `sum` = ~7
- Constants: `"rune"`(×2), `0` = 3
- Invocations: `filter`, `length`, `filter`, `reduce`, `+`, `runesBasePremium`, `+` = 7 invocations × 2 = 14
- Total ≈ **24**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 8 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Names are clear. One minor observation: `runesBasePremium` uses the magic check `runeCount === 3`. The spec concept is "exactly a block of 3 runes gets block pricing." This is already factored into a well-named helper with a named constant, so intent is revealed.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: `policyBasePremium` iterates `items` twice (one `filter` for runes, one `filter`+`reduce` for non-runes). This is a mild conceptual duplication (two passes over the same list), but the two passes represent genuinely different computations (rune block logic vs. simple summation). Collapsing them would require interleaving rune-counting with non-rune summation, which would *hurt* clarity. Rule 2 trumps Rule 3 here — keep separate.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: I considered inlining `runesBasePremium` into `policyBasePremium`, but the separation cleanly isolates the block-pricing rule. Keeping it.

I did look for one justified small improvement. The condition `runeCount === 3 ? RUNE_BLOCK_PREMIUM : runeCount * BASE_PREMIUM.rune` is correct for all current tests (0, 2, 3, 4 runes). Since the only future-facing tests (`7 runes → 175`, multiple blocks) are not yet written, generalizing the block logic now would be implementing ahead of tests. I will not do that.

## Step 4 & 5: Refactoring Decision

After systematic evaluation, the code is already clean for the current test set. Per the mandate to attempt at least one improvement, I evaluated every candidate and each one either implements ahead of unwritten tests (generalizing block logic) or sacrifices clarity for lower mass (merging the two filter passes). 

The one safe, clarity-neutral-to-positive change available: the two `.filter()` passes in `policyBasePremium` partition `items` by the same predicate (`type === "rune"`). I can name that predicate to make the partition explicit without changing the two-pass structure. However, introducing a predicate binding for a single inline use adds an element (Rule 4) without improving the already-clear `item.type === "rune"` / `!== "rune"`. That is not a net improvement.

**Conclusion**: No refactoring applied — the code is already optimal for the current 8 tests. Forcing a change would violate either "don't implement ahead of tests" or "Rule 2 trumps APP." I confirmed tests remain green (no changes made).

---

## Refactoring Report

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `runScenario`, `quotePremium`, `policyBasePremium`, `runesBasePremium`, `RUNE_BLOCK_PREMIUM`, and all local bindings clearly reveal intent for the behavior tested so far. No renames warranted.

**APP Mass**: Unchanged (no code modified). `runesBasePremium` ≈ 10, `policyBasePremium` ≈ 24.

**Four Rules assessment**:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): 8/8 green, confirmed via `pnpm test`.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Names and structure are self-documenting.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): The two `.filter()` passes over `items` are distinct computations (rune-block pricing vs. plain summation); merging them would interleave concerns and reduce clarity. Rule 2 trumps Rule 3 here.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): No unnecessary abstractions. Inlining `runesBasePremium` would bury the block-pricing rule; introducing a partition-predicate binding adds an element without clarity gain.

**Why no change was made**: Every candidate improvement was rejected for a principled reason — generalizing the `runeCount === 3` block logic would implement ahead of untested behavior (`7 runes`, multiple blocks are still `it.todo`), and the only mass-reducing structural change (single-pass partition) would sacrifice clarity. The Green phase added no production code (the 4-runes test was already satisfied by existing logic), and the code is already clean for the current test set.

**Tests**: All passing (8 passed, 28 todo).

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
